ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for 'finger food platter'; closest defensible match is appetizer food product (FOODON_03315091). Platter is a composite mixed-fried-snack dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes — composite dishes not elsewhere classified
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP used as the closest defensible code for a mixed composite fried-snack platter. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Filipino finger food platter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for mixed finger food platter.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of approximately 500 g, intended for 2–3 persons, typically containing a mix of fried chicken wings, fish balls, lumpia Shanghai, kwek-kwek, and French fries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mixed Appetizers / Fried Snack Composite; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food for individual components (fish balls, spring rolls, chicken wings)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite Filipino finger food platter. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted averaging of individual component data from USDA FoodData Central (chicken wings — fried, French fries — fast food, spring rolls — fried) and Singapore HPB database (fish balls, lumpia). The input record values (energy 240 kcal, protein 11 g, fat 17 g, carbohydrate 12 g, sodium 440 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where component-level data could not be reliably aggregated.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on mixed fried snack foods including spring rolls (GI ~72), French fries (GI ~63), and battered/fried items (GI ~60–70); weighted composite estimated at GI 65.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite Filipino finger food platter. GI estimated at 65 (medium) by weighted analogy: spring rolls/lumpia GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database), French fries GI ~63 (University of Sydney GI Database), battered fried items GI ~60. Composite weighted estimate yields GI ~65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 60 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 39.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 440 mg per 100 g; a 500 g platter delivers approximately 2200 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this platter; if consumed, request grilled chicken wings only, omit fish balls and sweet chili sauce, and compensate by eliminating all other sodium sources for the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 65 (medium-high), low dietary fibre at 1.0 g per 100 g, and refined-carbohydrate components (spring roll wrappers, French fries, sweet chili sauce) create a high glycaemic load of approximately 39 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this platter; if consumed, limit portion to 100–150 g, prioritise grilled chicken wings, omit French fries and sweet chili sauce, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g per 100 g, trans fat at 0.1 g per 100 g, cholesterol at 60 mg per 100 g, and sodium at 440 mg per 100 g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; a 500 g platter delivers 25 g saturated fat and 2200 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this platter due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium content; if consumed on rare occasions, limit to a 100 g tasting portion and avoid all other high-fat and high-sodium foods that day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Predominantly deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; saturated fat at 5.0 g per 100 g is at the pro-inflammatory threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried items in this platter; request grilled or baked alternatives, omit sweet chili sauce (nightshade-derived), and limit fish balls due to high sodium content that worsens tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Finger Food Platter is a composite sharing dish served in Filipino-influenced casual dining and food delivery contexts in Singapore. The platter typically includes a selection of deep-fried and grilled items such as lumpia Shanghai (Filipino pork spring rolls), kwek-kwek (battered and deep-fried quail eggs coated in orange batter), seasoned chicken wings (deep-fried or grilled), fish balls (processed fish paste balls, deep-fried or boiled), and French fries, accompanied by sweet chili sauce and plain vinegar as dipping condiments. The dominant preparation method is deep-frying, which produces a crispy exterior, rich savoury flavour, and high fat content. Nutritionally, the platter is energy-dense at approximately 240 kcal per 100 g, with significant fat (17 g), moderate protein (11 g), and moderate carbohydrate (12 g). Sodium is high at 440 mg per 100 g, making a 500 g shared platter a significant sodium load of approximately 2200 mg. Saturated fat at 5.0 g per 100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g per 100 g raise cardiovascular concern. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0 g per 100 g. The estimated glycaemic index of 65 (medium) and glycaemic load of approximately 39 per 500 g serving make this platter unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The platter is culturally associated with Filipino street food traditions and is popular as a party or sharing snack in Singapore's multicultural food scene.
common_user_queries: What is a finger food platter?
Is finger food platter suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers a composite Filipino finger food platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted analogy from USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB component data. The input record values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is strong and the composite estimate is well-supported.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fried-snack platter is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single entry; component-level aggregation was not sufficiently reliable to report these values. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the composite platter is not available in approved sources as a single entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite platter are not available as a single approved-source entry; USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB database consulted. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a delivery aggregator and is not an approved primary nutrient source; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB as the nutrient evidence basis. Ethnic tags 'Noodle', 'Coffee', and 'Cake' from the input record are inconsistent with this dish and have been removed. The dish is classified as Filipino cuisine adapted for the Singapore market. Halal status is not confirmed and is set to false pending verification.
